original_hypothesis.title: Early whitelist phase can be fully DoSed if oracle consult period becomes 0 (no observations), reverting all pool buys
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: UniswapV3Oracle.peek() computes `period = min(PERIOD, longestObservationSecondsAgo)`. If longestObservationSecondsAgo returns 0 (e.g., pool has no initialized observations old enough), OracleLibrary.consult requires period!=0 and reverts ('BP'). Since Whitelist.checkWhitelist calls oracle.peek(amount) for pool-origin transfers, all buys from the pool revert, freezing distribution.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is an operationally realistic failure mode during initial pool setup (or misconfiguration) that attackers can amplify by pushing the system to enable whitelist before pool observation history is ready. Even without malicious action, it creates a fragile launch dependency.
deployment_assumptions: Oracle pool has longest observation age == 0 at some point while whitelist is active
Whitelist is enabled in the token during this time (whitelistContract != address(0))
original_hypothesis.config_evidence: No deployment evidence ensuring pool has sufficient observations before enabling whitelist; UniswapV3Oracle.peek uses consult(period) where consult requires period!=0.
preconditions: Whitelist.checkWhitelist is executed for pool-origin transfers (from == _pool)
_oracle is set and callable but consult reverts due to period==0

initial_operational_evidence.suspicious_workflow: Whitelist enforcement depends on an oracle that can hard-revert under normal early-pool conditions.
initial_operational_evidence.admin_dependency: Requires configuration order: whitelist enabled before oracle pool has stable observation history.
initial_operational_evidence.exploitation_window: Initial launch hours; especially right after pool deployment/initialization.

operational_questions: What are the expected observation initialization steps for the chosen Uniswap V3 pool on the target chain?
Does the pool start with observationCardinality>0 and a nonzero oldest observation age?
validation_requirements: Deploy/initialize pool in a test environment, query getOldestObservationSecondsAgo immediately, then call peek; confirm whether period can be 0.
End-to-end test: token buy from pool while whitelist enabled; confirm whether transfers revert.

preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_1: Induce or wait for the pool to have insufficient observations (period==0).
preliminary_attack_methodology.phase_2: During whitelist-enabled trading, any attempted buy triggers checkWhitelist -> oracle.peek -> revert, halting buys.

potential_operational_impact.operational_disruption: Medium: halts token distribution through the pool until observations mature or config changes.
potential_operational_impact.recovery_difficulty: Low-Medium: owner can disable whitelist contract or switch oracle, but that changes launch policy.
potential_operational_impact.affected_operations: All buys from the Uniswap pool during whitelist phase.

execution_requirements_analysis.operational_timing: Most likely during early pool lifecycle.

investigation_priorities.on_chain_signals: Reverts with 'BP' originating from OracleLibrary.consult; sudden zero volume despite demand.
investigation_priorities.operational_anomalies: Users report inability to buy due to whitelist failures.

validation_result.impact_summary: When the oracle pool's oldest observation is in the current block (secondsAgo == 0), UniswapV3Oracle.peek() passes period=0 to OracleLibrary.consult(), which reverts with 'BP'. This blocks all token buys from the Uniswap pool during the whitelist phase.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The vulnerability exists in UniswapV3Oracle.peek() which computes `period = PERIOD < longestPeriod ? PERIOD : longestPeriod`. When `getOldestObservationSecondsAgo()` returns 0 (which happens when the pool was just initialized or the oldest observation is in the current block), `longestPeriod` is 0 and `period` becomes 0. This 0 is passed directly to `OracleLibrary.consult(pool, period)` which has `require(period != 0, "BP")` at line 15, causing a revert. Since Whitelist.checkWhitelist() calls `IOracle(_oracle).peek(amount)` for all pool-origin buys (from == _pool), every buy transaction reverts. There is no fallback or minimum period check in peek().
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: The proof_of_signal is verified. OracleLibrary.sol line 15 contains `require(period != 0, "BP")`. UniswapV3Oracle.sol peek() at lines 31-33 passes the potentially-zero period directly to consult(). getOldestObservationSecondsAgo() computes `uint32(block.timestamp) - observationTimestamp` which is 0 when the observation was written in the same block. No guard exists in peek() to handle this edge case.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: When longestPeriod is 0, period becomes 0, and consult() reverts with 'BP'. No minimum period check or fallback exists.

vulnerable_code_snippets.code_snippet:
function consult(address pool, uint32 period) internal view returns (int24 timeWeightedAverageTick) {
    require(period != 0, "BP");
vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: This require statement causes the revert when period==0 is passed from peek().
Returns 0 when the oldest observation was written in the current block (block.timestamp == observationTimestamp), which is the case immediately after pool initialization.
Calls oracle.peek() which reverts when oracle pool has insufficient observation history, blocking all pool buys.

calling_sequence: 1. User buys VULT tokens from Uniswap V3 pool, triggering ERC20 transfer
4. Whitelist.checkWhitelist() detects from == _pool, so calls IOracle(_oracle).peek(amount)
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The defect is in UniswapV3Oracle.peek() which does not validate that the computed period is nonzero before passing it to consult(). The getOldestObservationSecondsAgo() function legitimately returns 0 when the pool was just initialized (observationTimestamp == block.timestamp). This is a normal early-pool condition, not an edge case. The code lacks a guard like `require(period > 0)` or a fallback mechanism when the pool lacks sufficient history.

deployment_assumptions_checked: Oracle pool has longest observation age == 0 at some point while whitelist is active
Whitelist is enabled in the token during this time (whitelistContract != address(0))
config_validation.deployment_evidence: The deployment scripts (Deploy.s.sol, Init.s.sol) only deploy and initialize ILOManager and ILOPool. They do not deploy or configure Whitelist, VultisigWhitelisted, or UniswapV3Oracle. There is no evidence of deployment ordering that ensures the oracle pool has sufficient observation history before the whitelist is enabled. The hardhat.config.ts references hardhat-vultisig/contracts suggesting separate deployment for Vultisig-related contracts, but no scripts are provided for those.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The scenario is valid and requires no attacker action. It occurs naturally when: (1) The oracle pool is newly created/initialized, (2) The whitelist contract is enabled with the oracle set, (3) A user attempts to buy VULT from the pool. The getOldestObservationSecondsAgo() returns 0 because block.timestamp == observationTimestamp for the first observation. This causes period=0 → consult reverts → all buys blocked. An attacker could also deliberately ensure this condition persists by deploying a fresh pool and setting it as the oracle pool, though the more likely scenario is an operational misconfiguration during launch.
validation_result.impact_assessment: Medium severity. During initial pool deployment, all buys from the Uniswap pool revert when the whitelist is active and the oracle pool lacks observation history (at least 1 second old). This blocks the entire token distribution through the pool until the observation ages. The window is at minimum 1 block but could be longer if the pool has no activity. The owner can mitigate by disabling the whitelist contract or changing the oracle, but this changes the launch policy.
recommended_mitigations: Add a minimum period check in UniswapV3Oracle.peek(): require(period >= MIN_SAFE_PERIOD) or handle period==0 gracefully with a fallback (e.g., use spot price or revert with a descriptive error)
Add a guard in peek(): if (longestPeriod == 0) revert('Oracle pool not ready')
Ensure deployment procedures verify the oracle pool has sufficient observation history before enabling the whitelist

Consider using a minimum period constant (e.g., 60 seconds) below which the oracle refuses to provide a quote: uint32 period = longestPeriod < MIN_PERIOD ? revert : (PERIOD < longestPeriod ? PERIOD : longestPeriod)
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy a fresh Uniswap V3 pool with VULT/WETH. 2. Deploy UniswapV3Oracle pointing to this pool. 3. Deploy and configure Whitelist with the oracle and pool addresses. 4. Deploy VultisigWhitelisted with the whitelist contract set. 5. In the same block as pool initialization (or immediately after), attempt a buy from the pool. 6. Observe the revert with 'BP' error from OracleLibrary.consult.
uncertainties: The exact duration of the DoS window depends on when the first observation ages (at least 1 second after pool initialization), which depends on block time
If the oracle pool is different from the trading pool (which it appears to be - the oracle has its own 'pool' immutable), the observation history depends on the oracle pool's activity
Whether the Whitelist owner would enable the whitelist before ensuring oracle readiness is an operational question
additional_testing_needed: Test getOldestObservationSecondsAgo() return value immediately after pool.initialize() in the same block
Test getOldestObservationSecondsAgo() return value 1 block after pool.initialize()
End-to-end test: attempt VULT buy from pool with whitelist enabled and oracle pool having 0-second observation history
Test the boundary condition: what is the minimum observation age needed for peek() to succeed
